attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman presents to the antenatal clinic at 36 weeks gestation. She was diagnosed with Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV) at her first antenatal visit and commenced on antiretroviral therapy. She has been adherent to her medications. She has no other medical conditions. Her previous pregnancies were uncomplicated vaginal deliveries.

On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 76/min, blood pressure 118/75 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, and temperature 36.8°C. The fetus is in a cephalic presentation. Recent blood tests show HIV viral load of 1100 copies/mL.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate mode of delivery?
options: Vaginal delivery with artificial rupture of membranes
Emergency caesarean section at onset of labour
attributes.explanation:
This patient has a detectable HIV viral load of 1100 copies/mL at 36 weeks gestation, which is above the threshold of 50 copies/mL that is considered safe for vaginal delivery. The primary goal in managing delivery for HIV-positive women is to minimise the risk of mother-to-child transmission.

Elective caesarean section at 38 weeks is the most appropriate mode of delivery for HIV-positive women with viral loads >50 copies/mL near delivery. This approach significantly reduces the risk of vertical transmission by avoiding exposure to maternal blood and secretions during labour.

Vaginal delivery with artificial rupture of membranes would increase the risk of vertical transmission by increasing fetal exposure to maternal fluids. Artificial rupture of membranes should be avoided in HIV-positive women with detectable viral loads.

Emergency caesarean section at onset of labour is not ideal as it does not provide the same benefits as a planned procedure before labour begins. The goal is to avoid labour entirely to minimise transmission risk.

Vaginal delivery with episiotomy would increase the risk of transmission by increasing exposure to maternal blood. Episiotomy should be avoided in HIV-positive women unless absolutely necessary.

Spontaneous vaginal delivery would be appropriate for HIV-positive women with sustained viral suppression (viral load <50 copies/mL) on antiretroviral therapy. However, this patient's viral load is too high to safely proceed with vaginal delivery.

The management of HIV in pregnancy has evolved significantly. With appropriate antiretroviral therapy and viral suppression, many HIV-positive women can safely have vaginal deliveries. However, when viral loads remain detectable near term, caesarean section remains an important intervention to reduce transmission risk.
learningPoints: Caesarean section is recommended for HIV-positive women with viral load >50 copies/mL near delivery.
Vaginal delivery is appropriate for HIV-positive women with sustained viral suppression on antiretroviral therapy.
The decision regarding mode of delivery should be based on viral load at 36 weeks gestation.
references: Australasian Society for Infectious Diseases (ASID). Management of perinatal infections. East Melbourne: ASID; 2022 [cited 2024 Nov 5]. Available from: https://ranzcog.edu.au/wp-content/uploads/2022/05/ASID-Management-of-Perinatal-Infections.pdf
